1: "It is for freedom that Christ has set us free. Stand firm, then, and do not let yourselves be burdened again by a yoke of slavery" (tē eleutheria hēmas Christos ēleutherōsen; stēkete oun kai mē palin zygō douleias enechesthe) — the "yoke of slavery" (zygō douleias) was a recognized Jewish metaphor for Torah-observance: the rabbis spoke of "taking on the yoke of Torah" and "the yoke of the kingdom of heaven" (m. Avot Galatians 3:5). To accept circumcision and law-keeping was to take on the yoke that Peter himself described as a burden "which neither our ancestors nor we have been able to bear" (Acts 15:10). The freedom Paul declares is freedom from this yoke — not lawlessness but a different form of governance.
2: "Mark my words! I, Paul, tell you that if you let yourselves be circumcised, Christ will be of no value to you at all" (ide egō Paulos legō hymin hoti ean peritemnesthe, Christos hymas ouden ōphelēsei) — Paul's personal emphasis (egō Paulos) frames this as his direct apostolic voice, the most authoritative form of communication available in a letter. The theological claim about circumcision's effect on Christ's value is stark: circumcision as a means of justification places one outside the realm where Christ's death functions as justification. The two systems (law-righteousness through circumcision and grace-righteousness through Christ) are mutually exclusive as grounds of standing before God.
3: "Again I declare to every man who lets himself be circumcised that he is obligated to obey the whole law" (opheiletēs estin holon ton nomon poiēsai) — the obligation incurred by circumcision is total: the one who undergoes circumcision as a religious act is taking on the entire law-covenant package (Exodus 24:7; Deuteronomy 27:26 — cf. Galatians 3:10). This was the standard Jewish understanding: circumcision was the entry-point into the covenant that required Torah-keeping in all its dimensions. Paul's point is that the agitators who urge circumcision may not have fully disclosed this total obligation to their potential converts.
4: "You who are trying to be justified by the law have been alienated from Christ; you have fallen away from grace" (katērgēthēte apo Christou, hoitines en nomō dikaiousthai zēteite, tēs charitos exepesate) — "fallen away from grace" (tēs charitos exepesate) became a standard phrase in later theological debate about apostasy and perseverance. In Paul's context, the "falling" is not from salvation but from the grace-principle as the basis of justification: to seek law-justification is to exit the domain in which grace operates. Ekpiptō (to fall out of, fall from) is an athletic and nautical term — falling off the track or falling off-course from the destination.
5: "For through the Spirit, by faith, we eagerly await the righteousness for which we hope" (hēmeis gar pneumati ek pisteōs elpida dikaiosynēs apekdechometha) — the eschatological dimension of justification: the "righteousness" here is the final verdict at the last judgment, awaited by those who already possess it by faith in the present. The same apekdechomai (eagerly await with outstretched neck) as in Romans 8:19 and 23; the Spirit-given hope is of a quality similar to creation's own eager watching. The Spirit, faith, and hope are the three-fold gift of the present age that orients the community toward the eschatological completion.
6: "For in Christ Jesus neither circumcision nor uncircumcision has any value. The only thing that counts is faith expressing itself through love" (pistis di' agapēs energoumenē) — energeoumenē (present passive or middle participle of energeō) — "faith being made effective through love" or "faith working through love." This phrase became the center of the Catholic-Protestant debate in the Reformation: Luther's "faith alone" vs. the Council of Trent's insistence on love as the form of faith. For Paul, the phrase describes the organic connection between faith (the root) and love (the expression): faith that does not love is inert, but love that is not grounded in faith is moralism.
7: "You were running a good race. Who cut in on you to keep you from obeying the truth?" (tis hymas enekopsen alētheia mē peithesthai) — the athletic metaphor of running well (kalos etrechete) echoes Galatians 2:2 (the fear of running in vain) and Galatians 5:1 (standing firm). The sabotage-vocabulary enkoptō (to cut in, hinder, block the path) was used for obstacles placed on roads and for the military tactic of cutting the enemy's advance. Someone (the agitators — the specific identity left deliberate vague) has cut across the Galatians' path and diverted them from the truth-course they were running.
8: "That kind of persuasion does not come from the one who calls you" (hē peismonē ouk ek tou kalountos hymas) — the word peismonē (persuasion) is a hapax legomenon in the NT, possibly coined or chosen deliberately by Paul. It plays on the wordplay with "you were persuaded" (form of peithō) in v7: the persuasion that has led them astray does not have the character of the divine calling. The criterion: legitimate teaching produces receptivity toward the gospel; false teaching produces resistance to it — the test is not the teacher's credentials alone but whether their message aligns with the one who called.
9: "A little yeast works through the whole batch of dough" (mikra zymē holon to phyrama zymoi) — the yeast proverb is used by Paul also in 1 Corinthians 5:6 (in the context of the immoral man who was tolerated in the Corinthian community). The point: false teaching spreads by its own internal logic, requiring only a small initial entry to permeate the whole. The Passover-Unleavened Bread context of yeast as contamination (Exodus 12:15-20; Galatians 13:7; 1 Corinthians 5:7-8) may be in view: the new Passover community should be free of the old leaven of law-righteousness.
10: "I am confident in the Lord that you will take no other view" (egō pepoitha eis hymas en kyriō hoti ouden allo phronēsete) — the phroneo (mindset) vocabulary returns: Paul is confident the Galatians will adopt the right mindset in Christ rather than the competing mindset offered by the agitators. "The one who is throwing you into confusion, whoever that may be, will pay the penalty" (o de tarassōn hymas bastasei to krima, hostis an ē) — the indefinite hostis an ē (whoever that may be) is deliberately non-specific, possibly targeting the agitators' patron or leader whose identity Paul either does not know or chooses not to specify.
11: "Brothers and sisters, if I am still preaching circumcision, why am I still being persecuted? In that case the offense of the cross has been abolished" (ei peritmēn eti kēryssō, ti eti diōkomai; ara katērgētai to skandalon tou staurou) — Paul may be responding to the agitators' claim that he himself preached circumcision in certain contexts (perhaps referring to his circumcision of Timothy, Acts 16:3, or his vow in Acts 18:18). The refutation: if he were preaching circumcision, he would not be persecuted — the persecution itself is evidence that his gospel offends the circumcision-advocates. The "offense of the cross" (skandalon tou staurou) is the cross's incompatibility with the human pride in religious achievement that circumcision represents.
12: "As for those agitators, I wish they would go the whole way and emasculate themselves!" (Ophelon kai apokopsontai hoi anastatountes hymas) — apokoptō (to cut off, castrate) — Paul's most shocking statement in the letter; the phrase refers to self-castration as practiced by priests of Cybele (the Great Mother, whose cult was centered in Galatia at Pessinus). The priests of Cybele underwent ritual castration as a devotion to the goddess. Paul's irony is directed at the circumcision-advocates: if a little cutting is good, why not go all the way and castrate themselves in the Cybele-tradition? The comparison of circumcision-advocacy to pagan devotional mutilation is deliberately provocative and offensive to Jewish sensibilities.
13: "You, my brothers and sisters, were called to be free. But do not use your freedom to indulge the flesh; rather, serve one another humbly in love" (mē tēn eleutherian eis aphormen tē sarki, alla dia tēs agapēs douleuete allēlois) — aphormē (base of operations, starting point for an attack) — freedom is not to be used as a beachhead for fleshly indulgence, which would undermine the community Paul is defending. The Stoic philosophers also discussed freedom and the danger of licentiousness: Epictetus (Discourses 2.1.23) warned against the person who claims freedom but is enslaved to desires. Paul's solution is not law-restraint but love-service: the freedom that serves another in love is the fulfilled freedom.
14: The summary of the entire law in Leviticus 19:18 ("love your neighbor as yourself") echoes Jesus's own teaching (Mark 12:31; Matthew 22:39; Luke 10:27) and aligns with both the Hillel tradition and the Johannine tradition. Galatians 5:14 is one of three places Paul uses the love-neighbor summary (cf. Romans 13:9; 1 Cor 13). The use of the perfect passive peplērōtai (has been fulfilled) rather than a command to fulfill suggests that this is a description of what love accomplishes, not merely what it is commanded to do: love does not merely attempt to fulfill the law but actually accomplishes it.
15: "If you bite and devour each other, watch out or you will be destroyed by each other" (ei de allēlous daknete kai katesthiete, blepete mē hyp' allēlōn analōthēte) — the animal-imagery of biting (daknō) and devouring (katesthiō) describes community conflict in visceral terms. The warning is that theological disputes, when conducted with the weapons of personal attack and character destruction, produce the same result as the faction and division that the law was supposed to prevent — and do so to the destroyers as much as to the destroyed.
16: "Walk by the Spirit, and you will not gratify the desires of the flesh" (pneumati stoicheite kai epithumian sarkos ou mē telesēte) — the imperative to "walk by the Spirit" (pneumati stoicheite — literally: walk in line with the Spirit) uses the same military-formation verb stoicheō as in Galatians 6:16 ("walk by this rule") and Philippians 3:16. The negative result (not gratifying fleshly desires) follows as a consequence of Spirit-walking rather than as the primary goal; the primary command is Spirit-alignment, not sin-avoidance. The Stoic akolouthia (following nature/reason) provides the background, recast with the Spirit as the reality that nature and reason only imperfectly pointed toward.
17: "For the flesh desires what is contrary to the Spirit, and the Spirit what is contrary to the flesh. They are in conflict with each other, so that you are not to do whatever you want" (he gar sarx epithumei kata tou pneumatos, to de pneuma kata tēs sarkos; tauta gar allēlois antikeitai, hina mē ha ean thelēte tauta poiēte) — the conflict between flesh and Spirit describes the experienced moral condition of the believer in the overlapping of the ages: the new Spirit-life exists within the old flesh-reality, creating the inner conflict Paul describes more extensively in Romans 7:14-25. The phrase "so that you do not do what you want" may mean that the conflict prevents believers from doing the evil they want (the Spirit restrains the flesh) and also prevents them from doing all the good they want (the flesh restrains the Spirit).
18: "But if you are led by the Spirit, you are not under the law" (ei de pneumati agesthe, ouk este hypo nomon) — the Spirit-leading that characterizes the children of God (Romans 8:14) is the alternative to law-governance. Being "under the law" in Paul means being under its juridical claim and condemnation; Spirit-led people are not under that claim because they are not seeking law-righteousness. The Spirit does not abolish the law's ethical content but transforms the means of fulfilling it from external compulsion to internal movement.
19: The "works of the flesh" (erga tēs sarkos) list in vv19-21 is one of several virtue-and-vice catalogs in Paul (cf. Romans 1:29-31; 1 Corinthians 6:9-10; 2 Corinthians 12:20-21; Colossians 3:5-9; 1 Timothy 1:9-10). Such catalogs were a common feature of Hellenistic moral philosophy (Stoic, Peripatetic, and popular ethics) and of Jewish ethical instruction (Wisdom of Solomon Galatians 14:25-26; 2 Enoch Galatians 10:4-6; T. Reuben Galatians 3:2-6). Paul's list covers four categories: sexual (fornication, impurity, debauchery), religious (idolatry, witchcraft), relational (hatred, discord, jealousy, rage, self-seeking, dissensions, factions, envy), and physical excess (drunkenness, carousing).
20: The relational vices in the flesh-list (echthai, eris, zēlos, thymos, eritheiai, dichostasiai, haireseis, phthonoi — hatred, discord, jealousy, fits of rage, self-seeking ambitions, dissensions, factions, envy) are particularly relevant to the Galatian situation, where the community is being torn apart by the circumcision controversy. The word haireseis (factions, heresies — from haireō = to choose) is here in the sense of party-forming rather than doctrinal deviation; the factional spirit is itself a work of the flesh regardless of the theological trigger for the faction.
21: "I warn you, as I did before, that those who live like this will not inherit the kingdom of God" (prolegō hymin, kathōs proeipon, hoti hoi ta toiauta prassontes basileian theou ou klēronomēsousin) — the "as I did before" indicates an oral warning Paul had given during his initial Galatian visit; the letter reinforces what was already taught. Basileian theou ou klēronomēsousin (will not inherit the kingdom of God) — this is eschatological exclusion: those who practice the flesh-works as a lifestyle pattern are excluded from the inheritance. The parallel with the baptismal confession "the unrighteous will not inherit the kingdom" (1 Corinthians 6:9-10) suggests this was standard early Christian catechetical content.
22: The "fruit of the Spirit" (karpos tou pneumatos) is singular — one fruit with nine characteristics — contrasting with the plural "works of the flesh." The unity of the fruit reflects the unity of its source (the Spirit), while the diversity of its expressions reflects the fullness of love that produces all the other characteristics. The nine characteristics (love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, self-control) correspond to the Stoic list of virtues but differ in grounding: Stoic virtues are achieved through reason and discipline; the Spirit's fruit is received through union with Christ and the Spirit's work.
23: "Against such things there is no law" (kata tōn toioutōn ouk estin nomos) — the fruit of the Spirit generates a life that no law can condemn; the law has no jurisdiction over the Spirit-produced life because the Spirit accomplishes what the law demanded but could not produce. This is Paul's ironic reversal of the law-freedom debate: those who seek freedom through law-observance are ironically more constrained by the law's condemnation of their failures; those who walk in the Spirit are truly free from the law's jurisdiction because the Spirit produces the law's goal.
24: "Those who belong to Christ Jesus have crucified the flesh with its passions and desires" (hoi de tou Christou Iēsou tēn sarka estaurōsan syn tois pathēmasin kai tais epithymiais) — the aorist estaurōsan (they crucified) points to the historical moment of conversion/baptism as the act of co-crucifixion with Christ (cf. Galatians 2:19-20: "I have been crucified with Christ"). The crucifixion of the flesh's passions is both a past event (at conversion) and a present practice (the ongoing mortification that flows from the past event). The flesh does not simply die at conversion but its death-sentence was enacted at that point, with the execution implemented through the Spirit's ongoing work.
25: "Since we live by the Spirit, let us keep in step with the Spirit" (ei zōmen pneumati, pneumati kai stoichōmen) — the indicative (zōmen = we live) precedes the imperative (stoichōmen = let us walk in line with). The ethical imperative is grounded in the soteriological indicative: because the Spirit is already the source of the believer's life, the appropriate response is to align the whole lifestyle with the Spirit's guidance. The military-formation image of stoicheō suggests not mere individual moral improvement but coordinated community movement in the Spirit's direction.
26: "Let us not become conceited, provoking and envying each other" (mē ginōmetha kenodoxoi, allēlous prokaloumenoi, allēlois phthonountes) — kenodoxia (empty glory, vainglory) is the antithesis of the cross-shaped humility that Paul has commended throughout the letter. The two relational vices (prokaleō = to challenge or provoke to combat; phthoneō = to envy, to desire what another has) are the typical results of kenodoxia: the person who seeks vain glory either attacks others (provocation) or resents their successes (envy). Both are works of the flesh (v20: eris, zēlos, phthonos) expressed in the community's internal life.
